parts.text:
On the morning you were born, your great-grandparents were living in a world that felt both ancient and expectant, tucked away in a house where the scent of cedar shavings and line-dried linen hung heavy in the air.

Your great-grandfather, a man who measured his life by the rhythm of the seasons and the pull of the tides, was out in the garden before the sun had fully crested the horizon. He was tending to a row of heirloom tomatoes, his calloused hands moving with a gentleness that belied his rugged appearance. He had a feeling about this day—a strange, buzzing energy in the soles of his feet. He spent the morning repairing a loose slat on the porch swing, whistling a tune he couldn’t quite name, but which felt like a welcome.

Inside, your great-grandmother was engaged in her own ritual of preparation. She had spent the early hours in the kitchen, the linoleum floor cool beneath her feet. She was baking a heavy, dark loaf of honey-oat bread, the kind that fills every corner of a home with a sense of security. As she kneaded the dough, she looked out the window at an old oak tree and noticed a pair of blue jays nesting—a sign, she whispered to herself, of a spirited arrival.

By midday, the house was silent and still, the heat of the afternoon settling over the roof like a heavy quilt. They sat together on the porch, sharing a pitcher of iced tea sweetened with mint from the garden. They didn't speak much; they had reached that stage of a long marriage where silence was a shared language. They watched the shadows of the clouds race across the nearby fields, both of them feeling a sudden, inexplicable tug toward the telephone.

When the call finally came in the late afternoon, the house seemed to exhale. Your great-grandfather answered it, his voice cracking slightly as he heard the news. He didn't say much—just a few "wells" and a "praise be"—before handing the receiver to your great-grandmother. 

She listened with her eyes closed, a slow smile spreading across her face as she pictured a new branch growing on the family tree. After they hung up, they didn't rush to celebrate with noise. Instead, your great-grandfather went to his workbench and began carving a small wooden bird out of a scrap of pine, while your great-grandmother began searching through her cedar chest for the softest wool blanket she had ever knit. 

That evening, as the sun dipped low and painted the sky in shades of violet and gold, they sat on their porch once more. They toasted to your health with small glasses of homemade blackberry wine, looking out at the stars and knowing that the world was, from that moment on, a different and much better place.
